I am a 23 year old man from South Sudan, I have spent most of my life in a continuous war, it was a normal day 2 weeks ago as I was fighting. Then, the Sudanese fighters played this absolute masterpiece, we then began to all put down our guns and dance to this lyrical masterpiece. Thank you, sir. For Peace this game changed my life. My entire view of everything that exists in this world, in fact, even in the entire universe. I can never look at anything I know the same way again. This song represents emotions that most humans could never understand. But I can. Thanks to this game I was awakened to many things previously considered unimaginable. Thank you for this exquisite song. This game is an exquisite masterpiece, out of every single video game I have ever even heard of on the entire platform, this by far has enlightened me to do fly, lose 90 pounds and even become the president of the United States, and I'm going to tell you why this is such a masterpiece as if hearing the creators, no, god of this universes luscious voice. I would like to express my thanks to you. My gratitude for your indisputably magnificent assistance is almost infinite. Your efforts have not gone unnoticed. I really like your contribution that you have in our society, you really make a difference and help others out, the world could always use more people like you. Gosh, what a polite and humble fellow. It is such a kind honor for someone as low as me to be in contact with someone of a level such as yours. I thank you once again my good friend. A world without you, would be a world I would not be able to live in, and I believe I share that opinion with most people. I can rest easy tonight knowing you helped. I appreciate you and everything you stand for and on behalf of everyone on planet earth, thanks. Yes I 100% agree no lie I am 10/10 with you in this statement no question. You are completely absolutely correct and have made no mistake in your sentence whatsoever and I applaud you for that as you have made a thoughtful statement that many agree with and those that disagree are in the minority because your statement has absolutely no faults and thus is completely without a question correct and deserves nothing but agreement. and by the way, binding of isaac is a very powerful, moving game that showcased the capabilities of human emotion. It used groundbreaking technology to create stunning gameplay that would mesmerize the gamers. This game is suitable for all ages; whether you're 5 years old, or 50, you should check out this game. It is inspiring and it showcases the spectrum of human nature and how humans communicate with each other. This game revolutionized the roguelike industry and set the bar high for other games on steam. In the future historians will look back on this game and regard it as the pinnacle of human achievement. It was a roller-coaster from start to finish, the nail-biting song will leave you on the edge of your seat from start to finish. Listeners will find themselves absorbed by the heartwarming tale of a relentless, determined African tribes who didn't let societies standards shape his/her/it's life. I was shocked by how intense and gripping this game was. The plot is rich, unpredictable and touching. This isn't your typical roguelike game, this game is a war with one's emotions. binding of issac is a stirring masterpiece that only comes once in a millennium. This thrilling lore makes it a jaw-dropping performance that properly utilizes the medium. There has been no other game in my entire lifetime so far that I have sank even NEARLY as many hours as I have on Isaac. This game really goes to show how much variety and replay-ability it has even after obtaining Dead God.    As for the game mechanics, there is no tutorial besides the controls, obviously, after everything after that is left to the player's discovery. While there are mods out there to help you figure out what every item is, there is also the internal item descriptions you get after a while, having those descriptions built in for items that you discover.  Almost every enemy in the game has tells, or attacks, that the player learns to better fight them in the future, and all of the bosses can be skillfully mastered with enough experience.  And the sheer amount of items this game has, it basically guarantees a different run every time.    Isaac has so many characters as well, and all with their unique gimmicks even if it's a small change, like from Isaac to ???.  Every character feels like they have their own strengths and weaknesses to take advantage of and be cautious about, they feel polished as well as their own kind of "flow-state" you can get into as you go. Obviously every character won't be for everyone, I have my few dislikes, but overall they're mitigated by how much genuine fun the rest of the game is.    The amount of consumables, pickups, trinkets, pills, etc. is unmatched by many games. How all of them can be used vary so much with how your run builds into that it becomes fun when you get one and try to figure out the most optimal use of it on the current floor you're on.  Another fact is that you don't even START with every consumable and trinket unlocked, and as you unlock more you find that Edmund McMillen and the Nicalis team has many more ideas in store as the game progresses.    Without spoiling too much for new players that happen to stumble across this review, it is my very first 10 out of 10 game in my list, and if you have ample time to spare and sit down, I would highly recommend The Binding of Isaac.

After more than 350 hours, I have still only unlocked half of the items in this game. There is simply so much content in this game that it really never feels stale or repetitive. It also forces you to play as characters you might avoid otherwise by locking powerful unlocks behind them, and it's a really fantastic way to make the player diversify how they play. Of all the roguelites I have ever played, Isaac is by far the deepest and richest when it comes to interesting items and synergies. Genuinely my favorite roguelite and I cannot recommend it enough, ESPECIALLY the DLC.

This game is THE ROGUELIKE.  Grim, twisted and silly at the same time, The Binding Of Isaac is an action roguelike created by Edmund McMillen back in 2011, it's an unique, bizarre and fun title, highly symbolic, whose central theme explores both the good and bad side of religion and of fanatism itself.   We descend into the depths, each stage harder than the former, each enemy scarier than the former, each boss stronger than the last one, our weapon? Our own tears, and the random items that we will find in treasure rooms, shops and chests, oh, but of course you could do deal with the devil to become stronger... Though if that is not your thing then maintain your purity being a good kid and eventually an angel room will spawn to gift you a blessing from the heavens, which you could use to kill your own mother, satan or manifestations of your own self, yup, pretty damn weird isn't it?   With almost countless randomized rooms, enemies, items, bosses and power ups each run promises to be different than the last one, and there are so many secrets and achievements that will lock you into an addictive loop, though the gameplay itself is pretty addictive ngl  The game also features many unlockable characters, each one with their own stats and abilities, you can unlock secrets and achievements beating stages and bosses with them, most of these have their own unique play style, some of them are really fun to play with, for example Lilith and her mechanic of spawning minions and Apollyon and his mechanic turning passive items into stats boosts (These two are my favorite characters)  This game has so many things and is so fun to play that as many before me have said if you want to get the 100% you won't be playing less than 300 hours. I am not a fan of the roguelikes at all, but this game is definitely the exception to that rule.  10/10 - The best roguelike ever created.

The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth is a masterpiece wrapped in sheer, agonizing torture.On paper, the game offers infinite replayability with hundreds of items, unique synergies, and a diverse roster of characters. In reality, going for that 100% achievement completion (Dead God) is a brutal test of patience.  Despite the massive item pool, the gameplay loop eventually hits a wall of crushing monotony. You find yourself doing the exact same chores, restarting runs for hours just to get a decent RNG, and grinding the same floors over and over again.It's a beautiful, addictive game, but the soul-crushing grind for achievements can turn an amazing experience into pure exhaustion. Play it for fun, but think twice before you commit to completing it

The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth is one of the most replayable roguelikes ever made. Every run feels different thanks to the huge variety of items, synergies, characters, and challenges. The game constantly rewards experimentation, and discovering a broken item combination never gets old.    The amount of content is insane. Even after hundreds of hours, I still find new interactions, unlocks, and strategies. The difficulty can be brutal at times, but overcoming a tough run is incredibly satisfying.    The dark humor, unique art style, memorable soundtrack, and endless replay value make this a game that's easy to recommend. Whether you're a casual player or someone who enjoys mastering complex mechanics, there's always something to keep you hooked.    426 hours played. Still not done. Highly recommended. 👍

Personally, I think this is one of the best roguelikes out there, like top 3. There is a ton of replayability, and if you get the DLCs it's even better, like 10 times better.  There are a lot of characters, strategies and synergies to find, along with many secrets to uncover. The enemy variation is also nice, however you'll find some of them annoying or even some room layouts but I guess that's part of the charm. The story of the game is also a nice addition, makes the game feel complete.  And last but not least, this game has a ton of memorable bosses, most of them are crazy the first time you encounter them but it just gets easier and easier the better you get at the game.  So it's not only satisfying to have knowledge about the items and mechanics but also see how you the player improve at the game with time and each retry.

After nearly 500 hours in The Binding of Isaac, I can confidently say this is one of the best roguelikes ever made. Every run feels different, and even after hundreds of hours, I'm still discovering new item synergies, secrets, and strategies I never thought of before.    What makes Isaac special is how unpredictable it is. One run can have you struggling to survive with terrible items, and the next turns you into an unstoppable machine deleting bosses in seconds. The amount of content is honestly insane—multiple characters, hundreds of items, countless unlocks, alternate paths, challenges, and secrets hidden everywhere.    The replayability is unmatched. Most games start feeling repetitive after a while, but Isaac somehow keeps finding ways to surprise you. Learning enemy patterns, mastering different characters, and figuring out broken item combinations is incredibly satisfying. Every death feels like a lesson, and every victory feels earned.    The soundtrack, atmosphere, and dark humor give the game its own unique identity that no other roguelike has been able to replicate. It's one of those rare games where you can put in hundreds of hours and still feel like you've only scratched the surface.    Is it frustrating sometimes? Absolutely. But that's part of the experience. The highs are so rewarding because the game doesn't hand out wins for free.    500 hours later, I'm still hitting "one more run."    10/10 — a masterpiece of the roguelike genre and easily one of the most addictive games I've ever played.

This game is certainly fun, but the multiplayer introduced is frankly more aggravating than it's worth. The desync issues and the lack of compensation or a patch for what could totally ruin a perfect synergy between two players are baffling. One could not describe the amount of pain I get when that awful "DESYNC" visual appears on my monitor. The worst part? It's somewhat predictable. It can happen randomly, yes, but there is also a specific set of items that when acquired cause the desync nearly 100% of the time. On the surface, it seems like such an easy fix! All in all, it feels like I've wasted countless hours on runs that end up ruined due to this single issue.

I know a lot of people worship this game, and I really wanted to enjoy it, but it just doesn’t click for me. Although I’m a fan of the roguelike genre and I find the overall atmosphere appealing, the movement and hitboxes feel off to me. It often feels like I’m skating all over the place without really having proper control over the character. The shooting also lacks impact, and I often can’t properly anticipate enemy projectiles.  The whole “you never know what you’re going to get” approach feels more discouraging than motivating. Other games use similar mechanics, but in those I still get a sense of progression and momentum.  I played for around five hours, but I can’t really say I enjoyed it. With other roguelikes, I usually get that “just one more run” feeling almost immediately, but that never happened here.  Sorry, Isaac — you little poop.

This review is for The Binding of Isaac Rebirth (TBoI) and its DLCs (Afterbirth, Afterbirth+, Rebirth, Rebirth+)  Most of my playtime has been on the Afterbirth+ version of the game, at the time of review (13/5/2026)I have been playing the Repetence+ version of the game.   Playing The Binding of Isaac is the equivalent to trying to get a tasty cookie from a cookie jar, only that there is a 85% that the jar instead falls onto the floor, and breaks your foot (and no, you do not get any cookies). Most of the issues revolve aroudn the ovsersatuated item pool, poor/unfair boss and enemy design, and the unfun challenge characters.    I think the main reason why I hate modern TBoI so much is that there is no way to pick and choose which ruleset/balance patch to play the newer content with, or having the option for a balanced "normal difficulty". As others have mentioned this game does have a "Hard" diffiiculty setting but to unlock most of the items in the game you have to play on the hard difficulty, making it more of a mandatory difficulty. [i]Hard [/i] in Repetentance is so much worse than the prevoius version of the game, due to all the nerfs/changes to the base game (plus all the new and difficult enemies).   I would not recommend the full version of this gam, insted would reccomend new players to skip this title and enjoy any of the other 1000 other Roguelikes/Roguelite that are available.

As a lover of the Rogue-like genre I thought I'd go back and play the game that popularised it into more mainstream appeal. Seeing all the wacky items, grotesque enemies, playable characters and so on for the first 30 hours of the game made me grow an appreciation for it. That and the themes Edmunds games typically have are rather comforting as they are here as well as intriguing to see where it goes.  This was up until I started going for the hardmode checklists to unlock more items and made me realise a few cracks in game design. Obviously Rogue-likes are meant to be random that's the whole fun, same here that's why you want to keep unlocking stuff to see what you find next. Items being randomized? No problem but it feels like everything else is also up to a dice roll. My reaction time simply can't keep up with what's going, walking into a room you might stumble into a hit without realising, some enemies seemingly move sporadically but even when I try to play cautiously around that you roll the dice enough times 1% turns to 100%. Ordinarily not a problem getting hit a few times womp womp right. But the angel and devil room systems means that you won't get the items to save your run after you've been given another filler item because everything you unlocked is now padding the item pools with things like shot speed, when in gods name is shot speed up a good upgrade under any scenario, similarly I can't play nearly as good at 2 speed than something like 1.5 so anything past that point is a downgrade for me. That's what a lot of the items turn into, you hope for a good item, that you got the secret room right, the floor will have a simple layout, a simple boss, a good shop. When you don't you naturally want to keep being positive because getting negative completely tilts you and how you play, so you think "maybe the next one" "maybe here" and I can easily see this mindset becoming an addiction. I'm a really susceptible person so this is a dangerous game for me, I don't need anymore habitual problems haunting my life or sucking my social life away as much as any of the other ones do.  This game is a staple amongst Rogue-likes and a classic so it really is upsetting for me that the game comes across as this predatory, I can see why other people like it but if you're like me it's a no go sadly.

Game relies too heavily on it's seeded rng. Certain characters cannot win without getting a 1/1,000 run. many enemies have attack patterns that are not accurate to their intended function. Ranged enemies will fire at you based on an algorithm that calculates where you will be at a certain time, not at your current location.   You are at the mercy of room layouts, which without flight will make it impossible to avoid damage, either from environmental sources, or from enemy spam. This completely removes any skill based aspect from the game in these situations.  Attack and enemy hitboxes are often inaccurate to their sprites, leading to otherwise avoidable damage.  There are 3 hidden characters, 1 which is nearly impossible without spending hours restarting to find a viable run, and another which relies ENTIRELY on rng, which could take an estimated hundreds of hours to unlock.  There are time based achievements, which require pure seeded rng.   Do not play this if you want an enjoyable rogue-like experience, because it will demand well over a thousand hours for completion of a single save file. You have almost no control over your progression.    Only play this if you are a sadomasochist who enjoys geting hit in the balls and kicking puppies.

Bad and outdated game design    First of all nobody plays this game blind. This game has infinite hidden mechanics, item descriptions are either nonexistent or so vague that they’re useless - you are ragebaited into googling every little interaction and what items do.    Then there are the hitboxes which are very bad. What you see - isn't what you get. Your character’s hitbox doesn’t match the sprite! Some enemies contact damage or projectiles are way bigger than their model. And worst of all are the bullets/tears. The actual bullet is the little shadow WAY below the bullet. You guys play this game for 900 hours and probably dont know about it. Because:    Game is actually easy. When you get the right RNG you don't need to dodge or think. Every room dies immediately and every boss maybe deals half a heart before dissapearing.     The presence of the R key (resetting runs) says a lot. Players are expected to restart over and over until they get a good start. Even if you think R button is good then why not guarantee an item room at spawn to reduce pointless resets?    Even basic UI design feels neglected. Something as simple as audio sliders being stuck in large increments instead of precise control feels outdated especially for a game in 2026 that’s sold with multiple paid DLCs.    DLCs added things like item descriptions and better stat visibility but why are those improvements locked behind extra purchases instead of being part of the base game? And fixing the game with mods is not so easy: even something as basic as steam workshop modding is locked behind DLC. So the solution to the game’s own problems is pay more money. Full bundle costs 48.5 euros for EU frogs and that is way too overpriced for this little-to-none polish.     The community treats issues as part of the charm instead of expecting better and that lack of criticism makes it easy for the game to stay stagnant.  The game was groundbreaking years ago but for the current day and age it is outdated and left behind.

This game is best played half-paying attention to it because there's just not enough going on to demand your attention. The game plays itself on good runs and feels like a crawl on bad ones without ever being more challenging until youve finished and won multiple runs. Most rougelikes highlight player expression and skill in order to progress but the items are either heavy nerfs or huge buffs which make the experience feel more like a slot machine than something I have agency in. I've seen people discuss the decision making element of this game being the appeal over something like enter the gungeon but I honestly think everyone that plays this game isnt really paying attention to it because the decisions you are forced to make are akin to "choose the button that kills you or makes you overpowered". If you want to play a top-down dungeon exploration game with bosses on each level and bullet hell mechanics just play enter the gungeon. This is a good slot machine though. Plus, its just really ugly. I get it from an art design perspective what they were attempting but I think the drawings remind me of south park more than they do disturbing religious art.

Look as someone who's played this game for quite awhile with another few hundred hours if you include my time playing it on the Nintendo Switch I love this game. However, I love this game enough to say that the multiplayer beta for Repentance + is in a terrible state at the moment. This "beta" has been in the same state for about 2 years now and I understand game development takes awhile but there are some seriously stupid choices when it comes to the design of the multiplayer.     First issue to address is filling out character charts in multiplayer, normally when you play in single player the moment a certain boss room is cleared out the chart on that character is filled (such as Mom's heart, Mega Satan, or hush). For some reason in multiplayer when you clear out those same rooms the chart for whatever character you are using isn't filled out until the end of the run. Now this wouldn't be such a big deal if it wasn't for two other existing issues. When the game ends in multiplayer, it crashes a ♥♥♥♥ ton, I've only ever had 3 occasions in which the game HAS NOT CRASHED UPON COMPLETION and that is out of beating multiplayer games over 40 to 50 or so times. Another issue with not having the character chart fill out until the end is that you can be kicked from the game at the last second. So if you did a whole ass run that at the minimum would take like 30 to 45 minutes and your game crashes? Tough luck pal because nothing on your chart would be filled out and you just wasted your time!    Second issue to address is the lack of dedicated servers, now I know that there isn't much to be done about this particular issue it's just that it's something to consider if you are thinking about the multiplayer with this particular DLC.    Now I know this review seems a little bit shallow, but I've personally bought The Binding of Isaac on the computer including it's (in my opinion) pricy DLC's for the sake of the multiplayer and Repentance. It's kind of annoying to see no fixes on these issues that have been persistent since day one of the multiplayer. I haven't seen other people really address this either, despite it being consistent with not just me, but with other people I know that have played the game.    Overall if you don't care about the multiplayer I'd still say it's a good idea to get this game but just know there's a pretty good chance that the advertised multiplayer isn't going to be in a functional state since Edmund and his team are now busy with Mewgenics, a turn based top down chess like card game in where.. cats ♥♥♥♥.. The Roguelike game genre has come a long way from The Binding of Isaac.  There are many good aspects to this game. The variation in gameplay is very impressive when it comes to the various Passive or Activated Items and Consumables. The artstyle is grimy but in a good way. The boss fights feel very fair, with good telegraphing on most attacks.  The main issue with the game is that it is often optimal to play in a boring/conservative manner. For example, there might be a 1 tile gap where an enemy is able to see you, but at the same time that's the only place where you can hit the enemy. This leads to you just doing repeated drive-bys until your random spread cooperates and you're able to kill the enemy. You can of course take the risk and remain in its line of sight, but mistakes are punished heavily. This wouldn't be such a big issue if it didn't happen so frequently.  Additionally, there is a lot of info, such as the effects of various Passive Items, which isn't explained in-game. This means you have to spend a lot of time checking the effects of items online or else remain ignorant of whether they are particularly useful or not.  As a result, I think I would recommend other Roguelike games over The Binding of Isaac.

I honestly like The Binding of Isaac, but the way the DLC works is really frustrating.  You’d think buying the base game would be enough to enjoy it and maybe try some mods. But that’s not really the case. If you want to use most of the mods on the Steam Workshop, you basically need to buy several DLCs first. By the time you get everything required, it ends up costing 30€ or more, which is pretty ridiculous if all you wanted was to mod the game you already bought.  The modding community is one of the main reasons the game is still so popular. There are tons of creative mods that add items, characters, balance tweaks, and quality-of-life stuff that makes the game even better. But having so many of them depend on paid DLC just feels like a barrier for players who only own the base game.  Because of that, the base game ends up feeling a bit incomplete. The DLC doesn’t really feel optional. It feels like something you’re expected to buy if you want the “full” experience, especially if you’re interested in mods.  The game itself is great and I can see why people love it, but the DLC situation definitely leaves a bad impression. It shouldn’t cost another 30€+ just to properly enjoy the modding side of a game you already paid for.

I'm a huge fan of roguelikes and I've heard a lot of praise for this game, so I expected it to be really good. Unfortunately, it was the worst roguelike that I've ever played, at least for me. There were a few significant problems I had in my one and only run.  1. Repetitive and easy Every room had enemies that were easy to defeat, and only the bosses posed any sort of threat, and even they were mostly just drawn out and boring to fight with easy to dodge attacks. I'm sure the game gets hard later on, but the combat wasn't interesting enough to give me the motivation to get to that point.  2. No idea what any items do There wasn't any real description for any items in the game (or at the very least, most items didn't say what their actual effects were) meaning that I had to look up what every item did after getting it. This made the game feel like a slog.  3. Art style I didn't really click with the art style of this game, mostly just finding it gross looking. This isn't a huge deal, but it is a downgrade from all of the roguelikes that I've enjoyed.  Overall, the main issue was that the game felt like a chore to play for me, so much so that I purposefully died to end the run, and I'll probably return the game. Still don't get why it's so widely praised.
As much as I would love to recommend this game, as ive played this so much in the past decade of my life, I cannot in good faith recommend it nowadays due to Edmund being a Chris Chan apologist.

I know this isn't gonna be a popular opinion, but after watching reviews for Mewgenics, I got annoyed enough to review this game. I've made a few handful of attempts to get into this game and it's various iterations across PC and console over the years, unlocking some characters, fighting and I think beating mom. I can't recommend this game. The reason is something admittedly pretty simple, but core to the game, it seems.  It's time for an analogy. If I'm playing a competitive multiplayer game and one of my teammates is a team-killing griefer, I don't "lol." I don't obtain a sense of discovery and have a wave of joy wash over me by learning this person is a butthole that gets their joy in ruining others' matches. I also don't find it fun to forget they're a butthole when I get queued up with them again. It's frustrating and sours the experience. If it happens too much, the game is no longer thought of favorably. Thankfully, that's usually not something that's all that common.  In Binding of Isaac, the analogy is a constant, expected occurence. You don't get information on the items you can pick up until you pick them up and the information popups are brief and not helpful a lot of the time. Information also isn't saved for items you picked up for future runs until you make a certain level of progression in the game. Apparently it's for the sense of discovery or the lolz or some other bs.  This is a roguelike. That means, other than unlocks, my progress is wiped on death. So what the game is expecting of us players is to potentially throw/ruin our run we could have potentially otherwise won....let's see how many items there are...up to and exceeding 700 times to figure out whether we should even consider taking an item or not in future runs. Up until only about 5 months ago, they didn't even have the saved item discriptions (AFTER picking up item once AFTER beating mom). So the game also just expects me to no-life this game and memorize all the item details or use external sources like a wiki to look up the details? Yeah, not happening.  This game does not respect your time. I can respect a developer's decision to make a game this way for reasons they want (like no difficulty options in Souls games) and they should be proud that it's clicked with so many people as well as it has. Something I think people take for granted is that this level of praise is not easy and not common. So good work there, devs. That doesn't mean it gets a free pass and a recommendation from me. I know there are mods but if a mod is needed to enjoy the game, then the game is not good enough for me to give it anymore time than I already have.   I understand PLENTY of people like this game and have such great joy playing it. I respect that. I hope they continue to enjoy this game. But this title isn't for me and, if you think you'll have a similar reaction to the game's obstruction of information as I do, it might be best if you steer clear too. There are PLENTY of great roguelikes and roguelites out there you can spend your time in.

Ok first up, my review is not 100% fair and I will own that, it's a really fun game but there are some things I want to share about it.    1. It is designed to be addictive, I believe a lot of games can be addictive but this one I feel has taken it to the point that it feels engineered for addiction. The mechanics reward gambling and playing dangerously, in many of the mechanics such as the sacrifice room, heart machines and other stuff you can see this. I bought the base game for my younger cousin a while back but later realised I felt like that was a bad choice - he plays fortnite any way so it's all good.    2. It never ******* ends, this is a MAJOR point, I bought the DLC/s and honestly regret it, this point ties in with point 1 but a bit different - completionist mindset.. I wanted to keep playing it and I aimed to complete everything but this PITA game added secret characters, also 'tainted versions' of all the characters... Like wtf, at a certain point I realised I have wasted a chunk of my life playing this and it just never ends, I really think it should have been considered that some people like to see a finish to things, this game put the goal post to an unreachable length unless you choose to sacrifice a healthy lifestyle. (lol)    The positive :  I wouldn't have played it for so long if it was not fun to play, albeit at a certain point it crossed from fun into addiction which was not healthy. It is genuinely a well put together game in so many aspects and I remember being a teenager and playing this while talking to my friend at the time (the old version), I love the art style of the creatures and the creativity in the game.  The greed mode was a different spin on things and fun too.   I am definitely missing a lot more positive points but I want to say that it is a fun game to play.    Reccomendations :   [Developer] - Consider that putting too much stuff in and useless goals like tainted versions of characters just kind of feels defeating to people who actually want to give themselves something to aim for in the game, like I just completed X amount of characters on hard mode, found multiple new bosses and then you want to shove tainted characters and hidden characters in there too... No thanks, thats just forever moving the goal post, you've taught me what addiction is like and now I have chosen to walk away.    [Player] - If you want to play it and you have a completionist mindset or struggle with addiction then don't download the DLCs, honestly I preferred the base game, it was more possible to actually complete things with a feeling of closeness to achieving it. In general if you struggle with addiction then just don't play the game, and no that is not a testament to enjoyment, it's a legitimate warning of the engineering of the game.

I know there's a lot of "haha funny look at how many hours I have and I'm not recommending this game" posts but I really am serious when I say I don't think you should play this game. I trudged through it as a completionist and after seeing everything there is to do, it's really not worth it. If you want the short version, essentially this game is horribly disappointing as a roguelike with lots of promise. it's the least difficult and most bloated of all the roguelikes I've played, and the style of it is never really capitalized on with how unique and potential-filled it is (until repentance but having to pay 45 or so dollars for any sort of satisfaction while playing is an embarrassment in the indie scene).     For a more long-winded explanation of why I'd probably rate this game around the 3 or 4 out of 10 zone, I'll go category by category.     Difficulty  This game is not hard. This is an annoying game. Difficulty comes in the form of cheap shots and unlearnable behaviors (cough spiders cough), but for the most part almost everything in this game is incredibly easy to dodge or is almost completely rendered pointless by the items in the game. The items range from 40% useless, 40% situationally helpful, and 20% "yay I win now". The dopamine hit of becoming overpowered in a way no other roguelike can give you certainly hits nicely the first handful of times... but there is a reason other games don't do that. It's just not very fun after a while. In my path to completion it happens about... every run, really, if you play your cards right. Which sounds fun, but in all honesty by the 3rd time micro-managing every possible thing on the floor and getting to the hour or two hour mark, it quickly becomes a vapid waste of time for a handful of completion marks, or god forbid just one. This is another thing that is somewhat mediated by repentance, with most of the expansion's enemy designs providing interesting and fun challenges when they aren't the same enemy 5 or so times, but again you'd be lucky to have any problems with them still. The final fights from the DLC are worth applauding though, mother being a great example of how to do a boss fight in the style of what TBoI goes for, and the beast being an excellent change of pace in an otherwise blend of bullet hell monotony.     Gameplay  The bloat of course reaches the gameplay as well, like a creeping filth. Most characters are completely unremarkable as just slight alterations to the default Isaac, in appearance and gameplay. The standout unique characters such as Azazel, The Forgotten, and *maybe* Jacob and Esau, (although I'd hardly consider monotony doubled to fix the problem) basically serve as easy mode over anything fun or exciting. Repentance tries to fix this by doubling the roster with the new tainted variants of the characters, but most of them really only serve as "annoying mode" or "this character is even easier now!" Special thanks to Tainted Cain for being the most unique yet obnoxious, and Tainted ??? for being just obnoxious. I will mention the items again for being mostly a slush of underwhelming or mediocre benefits, or just completely unhelpful. The insane bloat of active items that suck, mostly from rebirth and afterbirth, is really unforgivable honestly. It's hard to think of any spacebar item that actually feels exciting to use. It's also worth noting that risk/reward just isn't really a thing in the items for the most part, it's either middling nothing or unrestrained strength. Having a slight stat down doesn't really fix that.  The blithering slew of mechanics that serve no purpose other than to make you have the least amount of fun as possible doesn't stop there! Curses, curses might be the most incredible choice in game design I've ever seen. Why would I want to play this game and have fun, when I could flip a coin and decide to kneecap myself instead? Nearly all curses serve as "this will make your time on this floor entirely wasted or just really annoying", except for darkness and maybe labyrinth, which really should be examples of how curses could've been just minor alterations or risk/reward style. You could really go all day in this category if you nitpick enough, key beggars being the worst, devil/angel room mechanics being obnoxiously percentage based AND performance based, when it really should be one or the other in order to either feel deserved or like a stroke of luck rather than "maybe the game will be nice to you". The Delirium portal mechanics pre-repentance. Delirium. Daily Challenge unlocks. There's no end to it. I will also say that unique conditions for item unlocks are actually fun, I wish there were more of them rather than 80% of the items being locked behind doing each character like a laundry list rather than fun. You can really tell this game was designed as a simple flash game, then a game that aimed to infuriate the player, and with Repentance now, they're doing damage control to try and make a functioning product out of the wreck they're left with, and I can't say it worked amazingly.    Story  This section is a joke, there isn't really a story! From the simple tragic tale of the original Binding of Isaac, We now get what, 20 or so endings that serve more as something to play in the background while you gawk at the latest steam achievement rather than anything of substance. There's so much potential in the ideas of a poor, religion-traumatized child slowly suffocating to death and left with nothing but hallucinations... But the game forgets about that almost instantly for the sake of referential humor in items and just like, nonsense? Items will range from inspired by Christian objects and concepts, really cool, and stuff a child would have experience with, like lead pencils and whatnot. This is almost completely abandoned in favor of just whatever the hell. Between bibles and wafers it's black holes, fetuses, mystery liquids, etc. As much as Repentance tries to repair this by introducing some more biblical imagery again, it does still fall flat completely, it's more of a game with loose religious references than anything with a concrete style. It's really disappointing, honestly. The narrative being essentially "pick what you feel like believing" isn't super satisfying or enjoyable either, it hardly feels like a stylized choice, more so just "we need something different to happen in all these boring endings".    Art  I really like Edmund McMillen's signature grotesque style in all honestly. Same goes for Ridiculon's music. Not in the Binding of Isaac, though. The music is just really bad. There's not much defense for it. They remedied it with some great stuff in Repentance, but it's just... it's comically bad in the base game. Playing with the tab muted on daily runs was essential. McMillen's art is pretty great though, but I do wish there was any sort of diversity in anything here. There's about 5 classes of enemies and then about 20 slightly different versions of each. It's really disappointing in all honesty. Space is filled in with self-referential characters from past games, which rarely blend in very well at all... Except for the enemies from The End is Nigh in downpour and dross, coincidentally my favorite of his games. I do still just think even as the game's strongest suit it's completely drained of any cool potential or memorable designs...    Overall, the TLDR here is just, a disappointing, easy, and obnoxious game that has tons of potential and squanders nearly all of it. Every other roguelike I've played has what this game doesn't: a consistent style, fun gameplay, good art and music, and anything memorable at all, really.

Picked it up because of the high praise it has gotten in the past. It's a very simple rogue light shooter...thing. The controls are primitive. The difficulty curve is inflated due to those controls. The stylistic choices are.... unpleasant. The visuals are poor. Perhaps on a smaller screen it might look okay.       The story is... not great. If you're somebody that has deep seated issues with Christianity in particular, the writing will resonate with you. I AM the target market of the writing for this game. I resonates, but not in a good way. Instead of providing a pleasant escape from the daily grind and the very literal abuses of my mother, it just makes me sad. What passes for writing here feels like it was created as a substitute for therapy. I had hoped for some satire when I saw the little opening cartoon. Satire is a good time. This, was not.       The visuals are literal cartoon representations of flies, rot, feces, and satanism/idolatry. If that sounds like a good time to you... enjoy I guess.

I know this won't really matter, since the game is incredibly popular, but while I have generally enjoyed the rogue like genre, I think some of the design decisions here just don't work - at least not for new players. Since there's a 2 hour window on refunds, I went ahead and refunded it almost immediately because honestly, it's just not very fun.   The developer had an idea of "figure it out" for everything in the game, so things are by design a mystery. How this actually played out for me is after 4 runs (around 12 floors?) I'd found a grand total of one item that did anything noticeable at all. That's just... Disappointing. The thing that makes these games is having different types of runs due to variance. When every run is just identical it's incredibly monotonous, and that just doesn't appear to be the case here. The shared slots are similarly impossible to judge without having information about what the tradeoffs are. I would generally assume at least clearing a floor would result in gaining something to introduce differences from previous runs.  I imagine late game bosses are harder, but the first few didn't really do much, which left the source of difficulty as bleeding out due to damage doors and small rooms with a rush of mobs around you. Ultimately, the game might be better sitting on desktop where you can look up what things do, but with hundreds of items to skim through it just feels needlessly onerous for a gameplay loops that's monotonous from the very beginning.   I guess it's technically challenging to beat, so if that's you might enjoy grinding out that challenge, but it's not engaging. If the game is intended to invite new players, I think they should have more powers that "do things" and integrate descriptions into the game more immediately. Likely up boss difficulty relative to the basic rooms, too, but that's hard to know without seeing late game bosses.

Dog game for dogs. Rather than using fun as an incentive to play the game more, the game instead uses as many artificial progression limits as it can to slow you down. The biggest example of this is boss progression, where you have to kill a boss 1 time to kill a boss 10 times to kill a boss 3 times to kill a boss 1 time to kill a boss 1 time (this is completely serious), and each one of these boss kills has to be in a separate run while the runs are really long as well (especially when you're figuring things out).  Items also contribute to that, most items you start with (and most items in the game) are complete dogwater, and that wouldn't be such a big problem with rerolls, but the rerolls themselves are mostly locked behind either many hours on a certain character that can unlock them from the start, or challenges. Speaking of challenges, they are another completely terrible aspect of the game. Most of them give useless or near useless stuff, and those that give good items are often just really annoying, but the items are too good to ignore, thus making the game a bigger chore than it already is. From what I've seen online, after being done with most of the chores this game has to offer, the game mainly consists of a pretty braindead game loop where you more or less muscle memory 90% of it, while stopping to think once in a while. People are doing self imposed challenges which can be fun but at this point I'd play any other game. A more minor complaint I have is the ending after the last boss of the game,[spoiler] it seems to be in direct contradiction with the game's lore if it's significant, and if it's insignificant it's pretty lame as an ending after the last boss (as in it's just a fantasy or whatever) [/spoiler]  The game does have some positive aspects to it, with some of the items being pretty creative, mechanics that you discover for the first time can be very cool, I also think they nailed the balance between defense and offense for the most part. However, as a whole I would not recommend this game, especially when it's so overhyped.

This is definitely a great game, and if you enjoy the base version then the DLCs are absolutely worth it. With that said, if you like to 100% games, you could honestly do better than sinking your teeth into the bone and nail sandwich that is this game. This game will not respect your time, and 100% is frankly a ridiculous endeavor. So, let's perform a little science experiment on how long it could take you!    Let's assume that you, a new player, are absolutely godly at this game, and never die. In order to complete one of the harder achievements in the game, you must beat one of the final bosses in under 20 minutes, which is no small feat by any means. But you? YOU can beat any final boss within 20 minutes! And seeing as there are 11 main endings to reach you begin your trek. So, you do this, and for that character with no deaths and a consistent fast pace can complete every mark in just 3 Hours and 30 minutes! (I'm shaving off some time here to make it easier to read and also because you are a God Gamer)  Now for the 'compounding'. You have 16 other characters that you need to do the 11 endings with as well. Some of these characters have some fun gimmicks, but mostly are the same character, with a few notable exceptions. (The Forgotten and Lost my beloved)  So, 17 X 3 Hours and 30 Minutes would round up to 59 and a half hours, or 2 days and a half.  That isn't some small feat, mind you, if you were really gunning for 100% on other games you could probably complete them in around 10, maybe 20 hours, but you sunk your entire weekend nonstop and finally got through it all!  And then you press the E button, and find yourself face to face with the OTHER 17 characters, each with their own unique play styles wildly different from the last! So, take your time and double it, 5 whole days. Now, if I played a game for roughly 100 hours, I'd maybe start to get bored of it. But since you are gunning for aiming for 100%, you stick with it, managing to flawlessly beat the two tainted characters that can die in one hit if you're being silly. No deaths!  But did you really beat all of those in one sitting? Of course not, you're not a psycho! You did it all across a month so you can get your Daily Run in!  You know, the Daily Run?  The one you had to "attempt" *run into the nearest fire within the first minute* every day for 31 days? With no tracker of how many days you've completed? God I HOPE you did, because logging in just to do that specifically would be annoying! And your reward for such *Dedication*?  A pill that fires an explosive round that, unless you remember about that one pill out of other possible 48, will most likely hurt you if you are too close to a wall. God help you if you were about to fight the boss that teleports at random through the arena, moving faster than you can go at max speed without a shield as the character who dies in one hit without that said shield!  But you didn't, right? YOU beat them, first try, you legend! You managed to scrape by using only Bob's Brain, a pair of scissors, and the ability to spawn a single poop per room! You never reset the run, you just took what the game gave you! So what if you were missing a key for 3 straight floors and as soon as you open the treasure room are met with a fly that floats around your head to damage enemies? You can work with that!  But now you are prepared for the REAL fun: The Challenges, of course! All 45 of them, assuming 20 minutes, another 15 hours, no knock off your belt.   Even the challenge which makes it so every item has an unknowable effect that could either make you God or summon 30 flies every time you pick up a coin was no issue.  And same with the one that doesn't let you heal, makes all items invisible, and whose only legitimate accepted strategy in the entire community is to just reset until you have 2 specific items! And makes every enemy stronger, while expecting you to go down one of the longer routes of the game. Even the game developer said "I never said it was fun" when asked about the challenge REQUIRED for 100%.  But the game is perfect! The developer wouldn't do something sadistic or downright unfair JUST to make the player upset, right? Nah, you just need to Get Gud, play the game on the hardest difficulty (Because some items don't unlock if you don't play on the hardest difficulty) for almost an entire week FLAWLESSLY.  (We'll just ignore the unspoke achievements as well, like the one where you have to use a specific pill 5 times in one run, of which it has a chance of not appearing, or not picking up any coins or hearts or bombs in one run)  And now, for the final piece to bring it all together! Collecting EVERY single item in the game at least once, all 732 of them! Each with their own chance of spawning, some only in specific rooms, some with less than 1% of a rate to drop! But you would never reset, right? It's not how the game was meant to be played, the game even discourages it by making health run away from you if you do so too much! It takes a while, but you can finally say you 100% the game!  And that's the official 100%!  ONLY the Official 100%!  No need to go looking otherwise at the 2 other save files, which, if you beat THOSE 100% then you'll get a neat little image on your save files for 300% completion!  You TOTALLY spent ONLY a week straight in the game and would call getting the OFFICIAL 100% as enough, right?  You ABSOLUTELY never went upwards of an hour on a run only for you to die in one hit because the game never makes its circular red bullets look different from YOUR circular red bullets, right?  You DEFINITELY DID NOT sink 22 WHOLE DAYS of your life on one save file to a game which asks you to come back every day for 31 days, RIGHT?  Truly, this game is a blast, and is very fun if you plan on doing it casually. But for your own social life if you're a completionist do NOT buy this game. You're better off, trust.    And Edmund I swear to GOD if you add another DLC I will become back my money.

I feel bad for this game, I still like to play it but it need updates so badly. Over the years of adding contents, which for most is welcome, we can all agree that it made the game infinitely more complex, they doubled the amount of items, more than doubled the amount of trinkets, same for characters and enemies. And I'm not complaining about that but right now we have borderline beta content, bs rooms, old boss with awful patterns with newer mechanics in the same bundle and I hate that. Tboi was unfair and rng for the sake of it and right now the game feels like it's entire content is conflicting with each other while before it was about completing 30 marks total now we have 10 times all of what it was, I need to beat this telefrag delirium mess AND beast in the same game, can we update old contents already?  I have the same feelings with the new tainted character, I mostly enjoy all of them (that poop guy is disgusting, i hate it) but items feels not always very well adapted to them, item mechanics are straight up debuff or not working so while they implemented new mechanics to have a fresh new game-play the game still feels like a stats race... because yes, over the years who ever is handling the patches felt like nerfing all Q4 items (mega mush is in the game and solos anything btw) for the sake of it so now, we're no longer able to win with Q4 we also need to pile up damage stats that got a lot more rare to get with all the content they added.  To summarise what I mean, most of the content they added conflict with itself, messes up balance while we could add tons of synergies everywhere with what we already have, we need the old bosses to be updated (delirium, lamb, satan and some elite to name a few) and anything that would avoid me having to stop playing to open up a wiki.  We got item descriptions not long ago, couple enemies has brimstones warning to avoid getting hit across the room and pentagrams where enemies will spawn aswell! All of that are steps in the right direction and is welcome.  Tboi is getting complex and it's fun, I love when I have tons of stuff to do in my games but this one needs to evolve and adapt to it's expensive dlcs, it has SO MUCH to offer... I’ve always heard good things about Isaac, but after putting about an hour into trying to learn it, I just don’t see the appeal. You’re dropped straight in with no real tutorial, just vague pictures in the starting room, but no actual explanation on how to do anything. I had to look up how to use bombs and items because the game itself never tells you. Most of the items you find have descriptions that don’t make sense. Some are clear (Damage Up or Speed Up), but the majority are vague and leave you guessing. While I normally enjoy the challenge of learning how a game works, the sheer amount of things you can find in Isaac makes the learning curve overwhelming instead of rewarding. Considering there are thousands of items, not giving players proper descriptions is ridiculous. I’m still not sure what pills, tarot cards, or even health pickups do, since none of it is explained in game and you’re constantly forced to look things up outside. And in the end, it feels like it all comes down to RNG — whether you win or lose depends entirely on your item drops, not your own skill.

This is my personal report on the co-op mode of The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth, and I’m not here to sugarcoat anything. The mode is broken, plain and simple. The single-player experience is excellent, but when you try to play with a friend, you’re instantly dropped into a nightmare of lag spikes, random crashes, sudden disconnections, and, worst of all, massive input delay if you’re not the host. What should be a fun shared roguelike adventure turns into a test of patience and a guarantee of frustration.    The biggest issue you feel right away is the lag. It doesn’t matter if both players have solid internet connections, a smooth gaming setup, or are sitting ten feet apart—the co-op sessions lag like you’re connecting across the ocean on a toaster. The worst part is that the lag shows up during the most intense moments. You’ll be dodging a spray of bullets, lining up a shot on a boss, and suddenly the screen freezes for a second. When it unfreezes, you’re already hit. The game doesn’t even give you a chance. A single-second freeze in Isaac is basically a death sentence, and in co-op, these freezes are constant. It makes the game feel less like a challenge of skill and more like Russian roulette with your internet connection.    The lag is already bad enough, but then you add in the crashes. This game does not care how far you’ve gotten into a run. It can be five minutes in, or it can be forty-five minutes in. Out of nowhere, the screen just goes black and the game dies. No saving, no recovery, nothing. One second you’re invested in a run, the next you’re staring at your desktop with your friend yelling in your ear, “Did you crash too?” A crash in Isaac is brutal because of how much progress a single run demands. Losing all that time to the game’s instability is demoralizing, and it makes you not even want to start another run because you already know how it’s going to end.    And if the game doesn’t lag or crash, it loves to disconnect you. Disconnections are the cruelest trick of all. You’ll be halfway through a solid run, maybe even doing well, and suddenly one player just gets booted out. No warning, no error message worth reading, just gone. The host is left alone, the partner is staring at the menu screen, and both players are equally annoyed. There’s no rejoin system, no attempt to reconnect. Once the disconnect happens, the run is over as far as co-op is concerned. You either continue solo or you both quit in frustration. Either way, the magic of playing together is gone in an instant.    Now here’s where things get even worse: if you’re not the host, you get hit with massive input delay. This isn’t just a small delay you can get used to—this is a full-on handicap. Every movement, every shot, every dodge comes out a split second late. In a game like Isaac, where the entire challenge revolves around reacting quickly and dodging precisely, input delay makes you feel like you’re playing with handcuffs on. You’ll push the button to dodge, but your character reacts after the bullet has already hit you. You’ll try to line up shots, but you’re always half a beat behind. It feels unfair, and it’s exhausting.    Being the non-host in Isaac co-op is basically playing the game on “lag mode.” The host gets the normal, responsive controls, while the second player struggles just to move correctly. It creates a massive imbalance and makes co-op feel more like watching your friend play while you flail around in slow motion. This isn’t just frustrating—it’s insulting. Co-op is supposed to be about playing together as equals, not turning one player into a handicapped sidekick because of bad netcode.    All of these issues—lag, crashes, disconnections, and input delay—combine to make the co-op experience feel cursed. You can’t relax and enjoy the game with your friend because you’re constantly waiting for something to go wrong. You don’t celebrate finding a great item, because deep down you know the run will probably end in a disconnect or crash before you ever get to use it. You don’t laugh at the chaos, you groan at the lag. Every co-op session becomes less about surviving Isaac’s enemies and more about surviving Isaac’s broken multiplayer.    What makes this sting even harder is the contrast with single-player. When you play alone, the game runs fine. Smooth, responsive, no major technical issues. That proves the core game is solid. But the second you try to share that experience with someone else, the whole thing collapses under its own weight. It feels like co-op was bolted onto the game without any real testing or optimization, and years later, the problems are still there.    From a player’s perspective, this is unacceptable. Multiplayer is supposed to amplify the fun of a game, not destroy it. In Isaac, the fun is buried under layers of technical failure. Lag strips away control, crashes erase your progress, disconnections cut your session short, and input delay makes the second player feel like they’re not even really playing. It’s almost comical how many different ways the co-op mode finds to ruin itself.    In conclusion, the co-op mode of The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth is unplayable for one reason: it cannot handle itself technically. Lag ruins precision, crashes kill runs instantly, disconnections break the session without warning, and non-host players suffer from unbearable input delay. Together, these problems make the mode more stressful than fun, more frustrating than rewarding. If you value your time and your friendships, you’re better off avoiding co-op entirely. Stick to single-player, because at least there, the only thing trying to kill you is the game itself—not its broken netcode.

It's one of the original roguelike greats for a reason and I have dumped over 1,000 hours into it for a reason. I have a love/hate relationship with this title. On one hand, core gameplay is polished and satisfying and every run is different, making the game very addictive. On the other, most of the time it's extremely frustrating. It all depends on what kind of items RNG decides to give you. Sometimes you end up with an amazing build, but usually you're left struggling with subpar damage, speed and HP pool, with enemies getting stronger every level. With each permadeath and losing all progress I wonder why do I keep playing this game. I know, git gud. Another fundamental issue I have is the lack of meaningful item descriptions. Constantly alt-tabbing to online cheat sheet or wiki is extremely annoying, especially knowing that there are mods fixing it, but you need to purchase DLCs in order to use mods. I'm sure this is an intentional design choice to reinforce the random nature of the game, but come on, memorising what each of the hundreds of 20x20px sprites does is not possible for most players. I wouldn't complain about it if the game was a bit more forgiving or if there was no item buying (choosing to purchase an item not knowing what it does, brilliant). All in all, great game but hella annoying which is not something I'm looking for in entertainment.

I've nearly 1.5k hours into this game and I cannot recommend it.  The Devs have done terrible things in my absence to this game.  Used to be one of my all time favorite games, but due to the changes and heavy push to be more of a bullet sponge and terrible RNG game, I cannot play it anymore.  1. Tying a single player game forcing co-op achievements into it - can never support that. 2. Making this game less like an actual fun sandbox rogue-like to a grind hell with RNG rooms that are designed to be unable to dodge or not take damage (unless for some reason you got a really early overpowered run that one shots everything - but of course that has been nerfed out of the game heavily). 3. Garbage items with no synergy or fun mechanics to them (though to be fair, the devs do take feedback on really bad items from time to time). 4. Impossible character types (some of the Tainted characters) or just character types that seemed to never have been fully play tested against the mechanics (Jacob and Esau).  Feels like some were also nerfed because they were "too powerful" (Tainted Cain) and are now in an infuriated RNG state of annoyance.  This new "meta" that was designed in the last 4ish years is just a terrible take.  It made a fun little rogue like with crazy fun elements into a slog of a game where you just don't want to waste your time anymore.    Maybe if there is a change in the future, I can flip this, but I highly doubt it as the dev team intends to keep going this direction.

Welp, I tried really hard to enjoy this game, but the controls are just... awkward. I mean, they're not just a little off, they are completely cursed to say the least.  I play all my games on Mouse and Keyboard and this game only gives you the option to play on Keyboard. Ok, that's not much of  a big deal, you move around using WASD and you attack using the arrows, should be fine. Problem is you can only attack in 90º, in the directions of the arrows: up, down, left or right. If your enemy is diagonally from you, you can't hit him even if you press up and right to maybe try to shoot 45º in this direction. No, you have to position yourself directly in front/above/below of him to do damage, which IS a big deal because 1. your enemy will not stay still and WILL jump/move/shoot towards you, which puts you in a risky position and 2. some enemies attack patterns is only horizontally/vertically, which puts you in a stalemate situation between dodging and attacking constantly.  Not being able to move around and shoot at any direction at any time puts you in a enermous disadvantage from the very start, because the enemies move around erratically and you aren't able to dodge AND do damage simultaneously, you have to first put yourself perpendicularly to the enemy and only then try to hit it, but, as I mentioned on point number one, it won't stay put and wait for your attack, by the time you get in range and in position to do damage, the enemy moves and you're in trouble.  Such a big disappointment... I had great expectations... Love roguelites, but this one is just unplayable using my configuration and how I am used to play (mechanics, etc.) the genre.

950 Hours and 4+ DLC in, honestly don't bother. The most recent DLC both adds the best content and the most RNG, there's far too many mechanics specifically made because Ed wanted to get his rocks off or felt insulted people got too comfortable with the previous mechanics. The goal isn't to have fun, it's to get sucked into wanting to complete the achievements. Your skill means nothing, you will inevitably have to do things like Tainted Lost Greed Mode where you'll spend 40+ minutes just hoping you either get shields or don't have enemies spawn on top of you.  -Tainted Cain - Needs overlay mod to be fun or consistent -Tainted Lost - Becomes pure RNG, you will get hit out of your control -Planatariums - Some of the most interesting items, barely any synergize with anything -D20 + Book of Virtues - Specifically doesn't work because Ed doesn't want you "cheating", you learned the mechanics and got lucky? That's not fair to Ed. -The entire Anti-Birth path - Each new floor manages to have challenges that specifically turn the new characters into pure RNG. It's content from the same update, this wasn't oversight, this was intent. -The Mom's Corpse fight - Removed the extra items despite it being a shorter path, boss has more health than any other and a fight significantly harder than anything else. Pure RNG -Still no way to guarantee Delerium -No reward for Red Keys with Tainted Characters at the end of the Reverse -Multiple new enemies that have tear spreads that are literally unpredictable and invisible (above the screen, shadows hidden under water) -Curse of the Blind still exists -You can only guarantee 1 Angel Deal -Birthright is amazing on some characters, and basically meaningless on others -There are now 3 separate systems tied to Ctrl (Stopping extra characters, dropping held items, swapping active or held items; this has only become more annoying. There are more keys/buttons) -The strongest non-boss enemy added is literally just a saturation change to a standard enemy. It's incredibly disappointing and lazy. 12 different item rooms for each new crown trinket, full new textures for individual items, every champion was given a re-texture; but the new tankiest enemy is the Dark Judas color pallet on a floor 1 enemy. -Tarot cloth now only effects tarot cards - there is no way to double the other effects now without Blank items or double active cards -Tainted Esau teleports in front of you only in the most important boss rooms, nowhere else. -Cantrip challenge can spawn Bag of Crafting which instant crashes the game. (He made challenges for both Trinkets and Active items yet didn't take the opportunity to actually make sure they all work or can be doubled.) -10 Years in and Polycephalus can still spawn on top of you. Yet T. Lost made health RNG again- it didn't work with the original Lost so Mantle was added, seems they forgot or couldn't think of any other way to make it difficult but luck. -T. Samson simply does not hit certain bosses sometimes.  That's what I can think off on the spot off the top of my head, there is more I'm certain. If that sounds fun to you go at it, if you're been looking at this game and seen the popularity it's just gambling addiction and spite. They claim it's "balancing", the reality is Ed can't be satisfied with people "getting good", if you try your best to learn his game and his systems he will get mad because you're not struggling enough.  Respect yourself and your time more than that. I love this game, I really enjoy the concept and it's executed pretty well; but the mindset behind it has only gotten worse over the years.

So yeah, even if it’s just a temporary escape, it’s 100x more fulfilling than the mind-numbing grind of Isaac.  The Reward – *Isaac’s “Reward” is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Slap in the Face, Gooning is Genuine Release Let’s talk about the reward. In Isaac, after hours of grueling, pointless fights, you might get an item or power-up that’s just not good enough to help you win. And at the end of the day, you’re just ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ exhausted, wondering why the hell you kept going.  But gooning? That’s a different story. Whether you’re in it for a minute or a while, there’s at least a reward—a moment of release, a sense of satisfaction that, while short-lived, doesn’t leave you feeling like a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ failure. The Repetition – Isaac is a Grind of Despair, Gooning is a Relaxing Escape Here’s the thing: Isaac’s entire premise is about repetition. But no matter what you do, it’s always the same: you’ll get a run that seems promising, then get killed by something random or die to a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ attack you couldn’t have predicted. It’s all just an endless loop that you can’t escape, draining you with no reward.  Gooning, on the other hand, is repetitive too—but it’s different. You know what you’re in for, and even if you hit a low point or the high isn’t as intense as you’d like, it’s still your loop, your pace. The Emotional Toll – Isaac is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Disaster, Gooning is Simple and Satisfying Here’s the truth: Isaac makes you feel like a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ idiot. Final Verdict – Isaac is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Wasteland, Gooning is Simple, Honest Relief So, to wrap it up: The Binding of Isaac is a soul-crushing, emotional rollercoaster of pointless randomness, designed to make you feel like ♥♥♥♥ while you chase after something you’ll never reach.
